name: Chesapeake Bay (Upper)
description:
The upper Chesapeake Bay is where the mighty Susquehanna River meets tidal waters, creating the legendary Susquehanna Flats — one of the most celebrated fishing grounds on the entire East Coast. Each spring, enormous schools of striped bass migrate up from the lower bay to spawn in these shallow, fertile waters, drawing thousands of anglers from across the mid-Atlantic region. The flats range from just a few feet to around ten feet deep, making them ideally suited for light-tackle casting and trolling for trophy-class rockfish.

Beyond the famed spring striper run, the upper Chesapeake offers outstanding year-round fishing opportunities. White perch and yellow perch provide fast action throughout the cooler months, while channel catfish and increasingly popular blue catfish inhabit the deeper channels near the river mouths. Largemouth bass thrive in the brackish marshes and tidal creeks that line the shores of Cecil, Harford, Baltimore, and Kent counties.

Havre de Grace, located at the confluence of the Susquehanna River and the bay, serves as the gateway to this remarkable fishery. The town and surrounding communities of North East, Perryville, and Essex offer numerous public boat ramps, marinas, and tackle shops catering to visiting anglers. Whether you are chasing trophy stripers on the flats or filling a cooler with perch, the upper Chesapeake Bay delivers a world-class fishing experience.

name: Chesapeake Bay (Middle)
description:
The middle Chesapeake Bay represents the heart and soul of Maryland's maritime heritage. Centered around Annapolis — the sailing capital of the United States — this expansive stretch of the bay spans five counties and encompasses some of the most productive fishing waters on the Atlantic seaboard. The main channel reaches the bay's maximum depth of 174 feet here, while countless tributaries, coves, and shoals create a mosaic of habitats that support an extraordinary diversity of fish and shellfish.

Striped bass fishing in the middle bay is nothing short of legendary, with trolling the main channel edges and live-lining around structure producing rockfish from schoolies to 40-pound-plus trophies. Bluefish blitzes erupt in summer, providing explosive topwater action, while croaker, spot, and seatrout fill stringers from late spring through autumn. The blue crab — Maryland's most iconic catch — thrives throughout these waters, and recreational crabbing is a beloved tradition in every waterfront community from Annapolis to Tilghman Island.

The Eastern Shore towns of St. Michaels and Tilghman Island offer a quintessential Chesapeake experience, with working waterman communities, fresh seafood restaurants, and direct access to prime fishing grounds. On the western shore, Annapolis, Deale, and Solomons provide world-class marinas, charter fishing fleets, and full amenities for visiting anglers. With over 400 miles of shoreline and countless public access points, the middle Chesapeake Bay is a destination that rewards anglers of every skill level.

name: Chesapeake Bay (Lower)
description:
The lower Chesapeake Bay in Maryland marks the transition from the mid-bay's brackish waters to the increasingly salty environment near the Virginia border. This region is defined by wide-open water, expansive marshlands, and a rich mixing zone where freshwater tributaries meet the ocean's influence. The result is some of the most diverse saltwater fishing in the state, with species ranging from striped bass and bluefish to red drum and flounder making regular appearances throughout the season.

Crisfield, proudly known as the "Crab Capital of the World," anchors the Eastern Shore side of the lower bay and serves as a gateway to the fabled Tangier Sound. This shallow, oyster-rich body of water produces exceptional blue crab harvests and outstanding fishing for croaker, spot, and flounder. On the western shore, the Patuxent River empties into the bay near Solomons, creating productive structure and current breaks that concentrate striped bass, bluefish, and red drum during their seasonal migrations.

The lower bay's remote character and relatively light fishing pressure make it a favorite among anglers seeking solitude and unspoiled natural beauty. The communities of Solomons, Lexington Park, Crisfield, and Cambridge offer boat launches, charter services, and waterfront dining that showcase Maryland's deepest seafood traditions. Whether you are trolling for rockfish, bottom-fishing for flounder, or running crab pots in Tangier Sound, the lower Chesapeake delivers an authentic Chesapeake Bay experience.

name: Potomac River
description:
The Potomac River forms Maryland's entire southern and western border, stretching from the Appalachian headwaters near Cumberland to the tidal marshes where it empties into the Chesapeake Bay. This remarkable river encompasses virtually every type of freshwater fishing habitat — from cold mountain freestone streams to warm tidal estuaries — and supports one of the most diverse fisheries on the East Coast. Seven Maryland counties touch its banks, and the river's proximity to Washington, D.C. makes it one of the most accessible trophy fisheries in the nation.

The tidal Potomac from Fort Washington downstream to Indian Head has emerged as a world-class largemouth bass fishery, regularly producing fish over 8 pounds from its vast grass beds and tidal marshes. Blue catfish have exploded in population in recent decades, with fish exceeding 50 pounds caught regularly in the deeper channels. Northern snakehead, an invasive species that has established itself in the tidal Potomac, provides a unique and exciting fishing opportunity that draws anglers specifically targeting this hard-fighting predator.

Upstream, the river transitions to a smallmouth bass paradise. The stretch from Harpers Ferry through the rocky gorges of western Maryland offers outstanding wade fishing and float trips for bronzebacks in the 2- to 5-pound range. Walleye inhabit the deeper pools, and spring runs of striped bass push upstream from the bay. Whether you are casting topwater plugs for largemouth in the tidal marshes near Waldorf or fly fishing for smallmouth near Cumberland, the Potomac River offers a lifetime of fishing exploration.

name: Patuxent River
description:
The Patuxent River is the longest river contained entirely within the state of Maryland, winding over 110 miles from its headwaters in Howard County to its mouth at Solomons on the Chesapeake Bay. This diverse waterway passes through five counties and transitions from a narrow freshwater stream in its upper reaches to a broad tidal estuary in its lower sections. The river's varied habitats support an impressive range of species, making it a versatile destination for anglers seeking everything from largemouth bass to blue crab.

In the upper freshwater reaches near Laurel and Upper Marlboro, the Patuxent offers excellent largemouth bass and crappie fishing in quiet pools, fallen timber, and backwater sloughs. Channel catfish inhabit the deeper holes and provide reliable action on cut bait and chicken liver throughout the warmer months. As the river widens and becomes tidal near Benedict, the fishery shifts to striped bass, white perch, and blue crab, with the brackish marshes producing outstanding spring and fall fishing.

The Patuxent River watershed is one of Maryland's most protected natural corridors, thanks to the Patuxent Research Refuge and Jug Bay Wetlands Sanctuary. These conservation areas ensure clean water and healthy fish populations for generations of anglers. The towns of Laurel, Upper Marlboro, Benedict, and Solomons provide convenient access points with public boat ramps and launch facilities, making the Patuxent an ideal day-trip destination for central Maryland anglers.

name: Choptank River
description:
The Choptank River is the longest river on Maryland's Eastern Shore and one of the most iconic waterways in the Chesapeake Bay region. Flowing through Talbot, Dorchester, and Caroline counties, this wide, shallow estuary has been central to the Eastern Shore's identity for centuries, supporting generations of watermen, farmers, and recreational anglers. The river's extensive marshlands, oyster bars, and grass flats create a rich ecosystem that sustains outstanding populations of striped bass, white perch, and blue crab.

Fishing the Choptank is a quintessential Eastern Shore experience. Striped bass prowl the channel edges and oyster bar drop-offs from spring through fall, with light-tackle casting and trolling producing consistent catches of schoolie rockfish and occasional trophy-class fish. White perch are abundant throughout the river system and provide fast action for anglers of all ages. In the upper reaches near Denton, the river narrows and freshens, offering excellent largemouth bass fishing in its wooded banks, lily pads, and submerged timber.

Cambridge and Easton serve as the primary gateways to the Choptank River fishery, with well-maintained public launches, marinas, and guide services readily available. The river's relatively protected waters make it an excellent choice for small-boat anglers, kayak fishermen, and families looking for a safe and productive day on the water. Blue crab populations remain strong in the Choptank, and a combined fishing and crabbing trip is a time-honored tradition on this beloved Eastern Shore river.

name: Susquehanna River
description:
The Susquehanna River enters Maryland at the Pennsylvania border and flows just 20 miles before emptying into the upper Chesapeake Bay at Havre de Grace. Despite its short run through the state, this stretch of the Susquehanna is one of the most productive and exciting fisheries in the mid-Atlantic region. The Conowingo Dam tailrace is the centerpiece of the fishery, where the powerful discharge creates a unique environment that concentrates massive numbers of fish in a relatively compact area.

The Conowingo Dam tailrace is legendary for its trophy striped bass fishing. During spring and fall, enormous rockfish — some exceeding 40 pounds — stack up below the dam as they follow baitfish and respond to spawning instincts. Smallmouth bass thrive in the river's rocky rapids and ledges, with fish averaging 2 to 4 pounds and occasional specimens exceeding 5 pounds. The annual shad run in spring brings both American and hickory shad surging upstream, providing thrilling light-tackle and fly-fishing action. Walleye and channel catfish round out a diverse fishery that produces quality catches year-round.

The communities of Havre de Grace, Port Deposit, and Conowingo provide excellent access to the river with public boat ramps, shore-fishing areas, and local outfitters who know the water intimately. The Conowingo Dam area draws anglers from across the Northeast, particularly during peak striper and shad seasons. Whether you are casting big swimbaits for trophy rockfish below the dam or drifting jigs for smallmouth in the rocky runs upstream, the Susquehanna's Maryland stretch delivers unforgettable fishing.

name: Deep Creek Lake
description:
Deep Creek Lake is Maryland's largest body of freshwater, covering 3,900 acres with 65 miles of shoreline nestled in the Allegheny Mountains of Garrett County. Created in 1925 by the damming of Deep Creek for hydroelectric power, this mountain reservoir sits at an elevation of 2,462 feet, giving it a distinctly different character from any other fishing destination in the state. Cool mountain waters, forested shorelines, and stunning Appalachian scenery combine to make Deep Creek Lake Maryland's premier mountain fishing retreat.

Walleye are the marquee species at Deep Creek Lake, and the Maryland Department of Natural Resources maintains an active stocking program to ensure a healthy, catchable population. Spring and fall trolling with worm harnesses and crankbaits produces walleye in the 15- to 24-inch range, with occasional fish exceeding 6 pounds. Largemouth and smallmouth bass provide excellent warm-weather fishing around docks, points, and rocky shorelines. Northern pike add a predatory thrill, with fish over 30 inches ambushing baitfish in the lake's weedy coves. Yellow perch and crappie offer outstanding panfish action, particularly during early spring and through the ice in winter.

The communities of McHenry, Oakland, and Deep Creek provide a full range of vacation amenities, from lakeside cabin rentals and resort lodging to restaurants, outfitters, and boat rental operations. Deep Creek Lake is a four-season destination, with ice fishing for walleye and perch being a popular winter tradition. Whether you are trolling for walleye at dawn, casting for bass at midday, or watching the sunset over the Alleghenies, Deep Creek Lake offers a mountain fishing experience unlike anything else in Maryland.

name: Liberty Reservoir
description:
Liberty Reservoir is a 3,100-acre impoundment straddling the Baltimore and Carroll county line, serving as a primary drinking water supply for the Baltimore metropolitan area. Despite its role as a public water source — which imposes certain boating restrictions including no gasoline motors — Liberty Reservoir supports one of the finest freshwater fisheries in central Maryland. The lake's deep, clear waters reach 135 feet and harbor an impressive diversity of game fish that draws dedicated anglers from across the region.

Liberty Reservoir is perhaps best known for its trophy tiger musky program, one of only a few such fisheries in the state. These aggressive hybrid predators grow to impressive sizes in the reservoir's fertile waters, with fish exceeding 40 inches caught each season. Walleye provide another marquee fishery, with spring trolling and jigging producing consistent catches along the main channel and deeper points. Largemouth and smallmouth bass thrive in the reservoir's varied structure, from flooded timber and brush piles to rocky bluffs and gravel transitions. Crappie and yellow perch round out the panfish opportunities, especially during spring spawning runs.

Access to Liberty Reservoir is managed by the City of Baltimore, and anglers must obtain a permit and adhere to electric-motor-only regulations. The towns of Eldersburg, Sykesville, and Reisterstown are located nearby, providing convenient services and dining options for visiting anglers. The reservoir's restrictions on motorized boating create a peaceful, uncrowded fishing environment that rewards patient anglers with quality catches and a serene natural setting.

name: Loch Raven Reservoir
description:
Loch Raven Reservoir is a 2,400-acre impoundment located in Baltimore County, serving as one of the three reservoirs in Baltimore's drinking water supply system. Despite being just minutes from the heart of the Baltimore metropolitan area, Loch Raven offers a remarkably wild and scenic fishing experience, with heavily forested shorelines, deep coves, and rocky bluffs that belie its suburban location. The reservoir reaches depths of 100 feet near the dam and provides habitat for a diverse array of freshwater game fish.

Largemouth bass are the primary draw at Loch Raven, and the reservoir consistently produces fish in the 3- to 6-pound range from its submerged timber, laydowns, and vegetated coves. Smallmouth bass inhabit the rockier sections and deeper points, providing hard-fighting action on finesse presentations. Like neighboring Liberty Reservoir, Loch Raven supports a stocked tiger musky fishery, and these toothy predators give anglers a chance at a true trophy catch close to the city. Yellow perch and crappie are abundant and provide excellent light-tackle and ice-fishing opportunities during the cooler months.

Loch Raven Reservoir is subject to electric-motor-only restrictions, which keeps the lake quiet and reduces fishing pressure compared to unrestricted waterways. The communities of Towson, Cockeysville, and Perry Hall surround the reservoir and offer full urban amenities within a short drive of the launch areas. For Baltimore-area anglers seeking quality fishing without a long drive, Loch Raven Reservoir is an invaluable local resource that punches well above its weight.

name: Prettyboy Reservoir
description:
Prettyboy Reservoir is the most remote and least developed of Baltimore's three drinking water reservoirs, covering 1,500 acres in northern Baltimore County. Surrounded by dense hardwood forests and rolling farmland, Prettyboy has a distinctly rural character that sets it apart from its more suburban counterparts at Loch Raven and Liberty. The reservoir reaches depths of 100 feet and is fed by the Gunpowder Falls, whose cold tailwater discharge below the dam creates one of Maryland's best trout streams.

What makes Prettyboy Reservoir unique among central Maryland fisheries is the presence of both warm-water and cold-water species. Largemouth bass are the primary warm-water target, with the reservoir's abundant submerged timber, coves, and creek channels providing excellent habitat. Brown trout and rainbow trout inhabit the cooler, deeper sections of the reservoir and the inflowing streams, offering a trout fishing opportunity that is rare in this part of the state. Crappie fishing is outstanding at Prettyboy, particularly during the spring spawn when fish move into the shallow coves and brush piles. Yellow perch and catfish provide additional angling variety throughout the year.

Prettyboy Reservoir operates under strict regulations, including electric-motor-only boating and limited access hours, which contribute to its peaceful, uncrowded atmosphere. The nearby communities of Hampstead, Hereford, and Parkton are small, quiet towns that reflect the rural character of the surrounding countryside. For anglers willing to trade convenience for tranquility and quality fishing, Prettyboy Reservoir rewards with a genuine wilderness-like experience just an hour north of downtown Baltimore.

name: Triadelphia Reservoir
description:
Triadelphia Reservoir is an 800-acre impoundment on the Patuxent River, straddling the border of Howard and Montgomery counties in central Maryland. Built in 1943 as part of the Washington Suburban Sanitary Commission's water supply system, this intimate reservoir features a winding, narrow shape with numerous coves, points, and creek arms that create diverse fishing habitat. With a maximum depth of 70 feet and 14 miles of forested shoreline, Triadelphia offers a surprisingly wild fishing experience in the heart of the Baltimore-Washington corridor.

Largemouth bass are the cornerstone of Triadelphia's fishery, and the reservoir produces quality fish from its abundant laydowns, brush piles, and aquatic vegetation. Tiger musky are stocked by the Maryland DNR and provide an exciting trophy opportunity, with fish exceeding 35 inches taken each year by anglers casting large jerkbaits and swimbaits along the main channel. Crappie and bluegill are plentiful and provide reliable panfish action, especially during spring when they move into the shallow coves to spawn. Channel catfish inhabit the deeper channels and creek confluences, offering solid action on bottom rigs throughout the summer months.

Triadelphia Reservoir is managed with electric-motor-only regulations and requires a WSSC permit for access, which helps maintain low boating pressure and a peaceful atmosphere. The communities of Fulton and Dayton are nearby, though the reservoir's secluded setting along wooded Patuxent River valley makes it feel far removed from the surrounding suburban development. For central Maryland anglers seeking a quiet, productive fishery with a chance at a trophy tiger musky, Triadelphia Reservoir is an excellent choice.

name: Ocean City Coast
description:
Ocean City, Maryland, proudly holds the title of "White Marlin Capital of the World," and the offshore waters along Worcester County's Atlantic coast rank among the premier saltwater fishing destinations on the entire East Coast. The continental shelf drops off relatively close to shore here, with submarine canyons like the Baltimore Canyon, Poor Man's Canyon, and Washington Canyon providing deep-water habitat that attracts a stunning array of pelagic species. From trophy white marlin and yellowfin tuna to mahi-mahi and bigeye tuna, the offshore fishing out of Ocean City rivals destinations far to the south.

Inshore and nearshore fishing along the Ocean City coast is equally productive. Flounder inhabit the sandy bottom from the inlet out to the nearshore reefs, and summer flounder in the 4- to 8-pound class are caught regularly. Sea bass and tautog stack up on the many artificial reefs and natural hard-bottom areas within an easy boat ride from the inlet. Bluefish and striped bass migrate along the coast in spring and fall, providing exciting action for surf anglers, pier fishermen, and boat-based anglers alike. The Ocean City Inlet itself is a legendary fishing spot, with strong tidal currents concentrating baitfish and predators.

Ocean City's fishing infrastructure is world-class, with a large charter fleet operating from the commercial harbor at West Ocean City and numerous marinas, bait shops, and tackle stores serving visiting anglers. The annual White Marlin Open is one of the largest and richest billfish tournaments in the world, drawing hundreds of boats each August. The nearby town of Berlin provides a charming, quieter alternative to the resort bustle. Whether you are trolling the canyons for big game or soaking bait from the surf, Ocean City delivers Maryland's finest saltwater fishing experience.

name: Isle of Wight Bay
description:
Isle of Wight Bay is the shallow, protected body of water that lies behind Ocean City's barrier island, separating the resort town from the mainland of Worcester County. Despite its modest depth — rarely exceeding 10 feet — this bay is remarkably productive, serving as a nursery ground for numerous fish species and supporting a thriving recreational fishery that is accessible to anglers of all experience levels. The bay's extensive grass beds, sandy flats, and marsh edges create prime habitat for flounder, bluefish, and blue crab.

Flounder fishing in Isle of Wight Bay is a local favorite, with summer flounder moving into the bay's channels and flats to feed on killfish and minnows during the warmer months. Drifting with live minnows or Gulp baits along the channel edges produces consistent catches. Bluefish and striped bass patrol the bay during their seasonal migrations, providing exciting light-tackle action on small plugs and soft plastics. Croaker and spot are abundant in summer, making Isle of Wight Bay an excellent destination for families and young anglers looking for steady action. Blue crabbing is outstanding throughout the bay, with chicken-necking from a pier or running a trotline among the most popular Chesapeake region traditions.

Isle of Wight Bay's calm, protected waters make it ideal for kayak fishing, small-boat excursions, and stand-up paddleboard fishing — activities that have grown enormously popular in recent years. Ocean City and West Ocean City provide convenient access via numerous public launches and rental facilities. For anglers looking to escape the ocean swells or simply enjoy a relaxing day on calm water with reliable fishing, Isle of Wight Bay is a perfect complement to the offshore action available just through the inlet.

name: Assateague Island
description:
Assateague Island is a 37-mile barrier island stretching along Maryland's Atlantic coast, famous for its wild ponies, pristine beaches, and world-class surf fishing. Managed jointly as Assateague Island National Seashore and Assateague State Park, this undeveloped island offers a fishing experience that feels truly wild and remote, despite being just minutes south of Ocean City. The island's surf zone, back bays, and nearshore waters support a remarkable diversity of saltwater species that draws dedicated surf anglers from across the mid-Atlantic region.

Surf fishing on Assateague is legendary, with fall bringing massive runs of striped bass and bluefish that can be reached from the beach with standard surf tackle. Red drum — including trophy-class fish exceeding 40 inches — cruise the island's surf zone and shallow flats, providing one of the northernmost reliable red drum fisheries on the East Coast. Flounder, spot, croaker, and kingfish round out the summer catch, with consistent action available from the beach and the back-bay shorelines. The island's over-sand vehicle zones allow four-wheel-drive access to remote stretches of beach where fishing pressure is minimal.

Assateague Island requires a National Park Service or state park entrance fee, and over-sand vehicle permits are available for anglers who want to drive the beach. The nearby town of Berlin and adjacent Ocean City provide full services, bait, and tackle. Camping on the island — either in developed campgrounds or at backcountry beach campsites — allows anglers to fish the productive dawn and dusk hours without a long commute. For surf fishing enthusiasts, Assateague Island represents one of the finest beach fishing destinations on the Atlantic Coast.

name: Nanticoke River
description:
The Nanticoke River is one of Maryland's Eastern Shore hidden gems, flowing through Wicomico and Dorchester counties before emptying into Tangier Sound and the lower Chesapeake Bay. This slow-moving, tea-colored river winds through extensive marshlands and cypress swamps that give it a distinctly Southern feel, more reminiscent of a Carolina blackwater river than a typical mid-Atlantic waterway. The Nanticoke's remote character and relatively light fishing pressure make it a favorite among anglers who value solitude and unspoiled natural beauty.

Largemouth bass are the Nanticoke's premier game fish, thriving in the river's abundant fallen timber, lily pads, and marsh grass edges. Fish in the 3- to 5-pound range are common, and the river produces occasional bass exceeding 7 pounds. Northern snakehead have established a growing population in the Nanticoke system, adding an exciting and controversial dimension to the fishery. White perch and crappie provide excellent panfish action, particularly during spring spawning runs in the tributary creeks. Channel catfish inhabit the deeper bends and holes, and blue crab populations are healthy in the lower tidal reaches.

Salisbury, the largest city on the Eastern Shore, provides full services and convenient access to the upper Nanticoke, while the small historic towns of Vienna and Sharptown offer quiet launch points closer to the river's most scenic stretches. The Nanticoke River is well-suited to kayak and canoe fishing, with its calm waters and abundant wildlife — including bald eagles, ospreys, and river otters — making every trip a memorable outdoor experience.

name: Chester River
description:
The Chester River is the principal waterway of Maryland's upper Eastern Shore, flowing through Kent and Queen Anne's counties before emptying into the Chesapeake Bay near Rock Hall. This broad, scenic estuary is steeped in colonial history and Eastern Shore charm, with gracious waterfront towns, working farms reaching to the water's edge, and a fishing tradition that stretches back centuries. The river's mix of tidal saltwater and freshwater influences creates diverse habitat that supports both estuarine and freshwater species.

Striped bass are the Chester River's signature game fish, with spring and fall runs bringing schools of rockfish into the river's channel edges, points, and tributary mouths. Light-tackle casting with jigs and soft plastics is particularly effective, and fly-fishing for stripers has a devoted following on the Chester. White perch are exceptionally abundant and provide fast, reliable action on small jigs, spinners, and live bait throughout the season. The upper river near Millington transitions to fresh water, offering quality largemouth bass and crappie fishing in a quieter, more intimate setting. Channel catfish and blue crab are additional draws that keep anglers returning throughout the year.

Chestertown, home to Washington College, is a beautifully preserved colonial town that serves as the cultural heart of the Chester River region. Rock Hall, situated at the river's mouth on the Chesapeake Bay, is a working waterman's community with excellent marinas, charter services, and seafood restaurants. Centreville, the Queen Anne's County seat, provides additional access to the river's southern reaches. The Chester River's combination of outstanding fishing, natural beauty, and historic Eastern Shore character makes it one of Maryland's most rewarding waterways to explore.

name: Severn River
description:
The Severn River flows through the heart of Anne Arundel County and empties into the Chesapeake Bay at Annapolis, passing directly alongside the historic campus of the United States Naval Academy. Despite being one of the shorter rivers on Maryland's western shore, the Severn punches well above its weight as a recreational fishery, offering surprisingly productive fishing within minutes of Annapolis and the surrounding suburban communities of Severna Park and Arnold. The river's 20 miles of shoreline encompass a mix of residential waterfront, wooded bluffs, and tidal marshes that provide varied habitat for Chesapeake Bay species.

Striped bass are the main event on the Severn River, and the waterway serves as an important spring staging area for rockfish moving toward their spawning grounds. Trolling, jigging, and live-lining all produce catches from spring through late fall, with fish ranging from schoolies to keepers in the 20- to 30-inch class. White perch are abundant year-round and provide fast action on light tackle, making them a favorite target for casual anglers and families. Bluefish push into the river during summer, delivering explosive strikes on topwater lures and cut bait. Croaker, spot, and blue crab round out the seasonal catch and give anglers additional reasons to get on the water.

The Severn River's proximity to Annapolis — Maryland's capital city and one of the premier sailing and boating destinations on the East Coast — means that anglers have access to an exceptional range of marinas, boat launches, and waterfront amenities. Severna Park and Arnold add convenient suburban launch points on the upper river. While the Severn may lack the vast scale of the open Chesapeake Bay, its protected waters, reliable fishing, and unbeatable convenience make it one of the most popular and accessible fisheries in the Annapolis region.
